metadata.title: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = complex / phase / real winding; CRIMSON = probability density / |psi|^2 / real-only attempt
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Schrodinger-equation consistency proof, no momentum-operator Fourier argument, no continuity equation.

beats.narration_text: Take a wave packet moving right and one moving left. Their probability clouds look identical. The thing that tells them apart is hiding in the imaginary part.

card.copy: Why the Wave Function Has to Be Complex

beats.narration_text: Measure the probability density of an electron moving right, then one moving left at the same speed. The histograms match exactly — both are the same Gaussian hump.
Classical physics says position tells you everything. But these two particles are different — one is racing right, the other left. The probability density is blind to that.

card.copy: Two identical probability humps. One moves right, one moves left. How does the wave function know which is which?

beats.narration_text: The wave function is a complex number at every point — a length and a direction. If you plot the real and imaginary parts as a helix in space, the helix winds clockwise for rightward motion and counterclockwise for leftward motion.
The probability density |psi| squared only uses the length of that complex number — it loses the winding direction entirely. Both particles have the same length profile, so they look the same.

Now try to build a wave packet from only real numbers. A real wave is a symmetric standing wave — it can't represent a single direction of travel. Any real packet is secretly two waves moving in opposite directions at once.
The imaginary part is the only place where direction of motion lives. The phase — the winding rate of the helix — is the momentum. Without the imaginary part, momentum is ambiguous.

This is why the Schrodinger equation uses i on the left side — it needs to distinguish future from past and right-mover from left-mover. Without i, time evolution would be symmetric forward and backward and every moving state would be its own mirror image.

Illustrative example: a Gaussian packet with momentum k-zero equals 5 per nanometer. The phase winds at 5 cycles per nanometer — clockwise viewed from the front. Flip k-zero to minus 5 and the helix flips. The probability density is identical in both cases.
The real part shows where the particle is. The imaginary part shows where it is going. Strip away the imaginary part and you can't tell left from right.

card.copy: Real part: where it is. Imaginary part: where it is going. Strip the imaginary part and you lose the direction.
